title: About Russell Johnston
text: Hello, I am Russell Johnston a current student persuing a degrees in Sports Media and Broadcasting at the University of Nebraska. I Have been privelaged enough to be able to coach high school football at a local school. In coaching football, you learn how much numbers are involved in the game, from yards to inches, and most importantly statistics after the fact. However, numbers dont automatically give you what your looking for until you dive deeper or look at the bigger picture.

title: The Inconsistant Airraid of the Cardiac Bears.
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(nflfastR)
library(ggplot2)
library(gt)
library(patchwork)
library(dplyr)
library(readr)

pbp2025<-load_pbp(2025)

bearspass<-pbp2025 |>
  filter(posteam == "CHI",
  play_type =="pass"
  )|>
  arrange(desc(yards_gained))|>
select(posteam,week,down,yards_gained,yards_after_catch,air_yards,shotgun,score_differential,season_type,side_of_field,pass_length,pass_location,pass_attempt,pass_touchdown,game_date,pass_attempt, game_half,complete_pass,incomplete_pass,qtr)

ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data = bearspass,
    aes(x = week, y = yards_gained, size = pass_attempt)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Week",
    y = "Total Passing Yards",
    title = "Chicago Bears Passing Production Over Time"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()











Code
bearstable <- bearspass |>
  group_by(week) |>
  summarize(
    attempts = n(),
    completions = sum(complete_pass, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    completion_pct = completions / attempts
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(completion_pct))

bearstable |> 
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(
    week = "Week",
    attempts = "Passing Attempts",
    completions = "Completions",
    completion_pct = "Completion Percentage"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Chicago Bears Completion Percentage by Week",   
    subtitle = "Completion percentage fluctuated throughout the season without a clear upward trend."  
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Source: NFL play-by-play data via nflverse" 
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Credit: Russell Johnston" 
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black",size = "x-large", weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_title(groups = "title")    
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(size = "medium"),
    locations = cells_title(groups = "subtitle")  
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(size = "small"),
    locations = cells_source_notes()              
  )








Chicago Bears Completion Percentage by Week


Completion percentage fluctuated throughout the season without a clear upward trend.

Credit: Russell Johnston










Code
ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data = bearspass,
    aes(
      x = factor(week),
      y = yards_gained,
      color = pass_length
    ),
    alpha = 0.6,
    position = position_jitter(width = 0.4)
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ qtr, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Chicago Bears Passing Yards by Week and Quarter",
    subtitle = "Each dot represents a pass play. Colors indicate pass length.",
    x = "Week of the Season",
    y = "Yards Gained on the Play",
    color = "Pass Length",
    caption = "Source: NFL play-by-play data via nflverse\nCredit: Rusty Johnston"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),   
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),               
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),                 
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11),                   
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 9)                  
  )









There are a couple of trends we can see. This graph shows the pass length of each quarter per week. We can see the bears struggled in the 2nd quarter with throwing long passes. If we look at qaurter 4 we can see a surge in shorter passses as we got later into the season. This graph shows a 5th qaurter for the two games the Bears played in overtime going 1 and 1. In week 16 the Bears threw too passes in overtime to win against an absoulute poverty of a franchise the Green Bay Packers. The bears saw the most distance throughout the season in the 4th quarter. This chart also highlights the inconsitency of deep balls througout the season. While there was an occasional deep ball the Bears clearly focused on short yardage and the idea of marching down the feild. The spread of data in the 4th qaurter reflects the Bears were either in a close game or losing. The 2025 Bears got the nickname “cardiac Bears” when they came back and won 7 of their 12 victories while trailing with 2 minutes left in the fourth.


Code
bearswaf <-data.frame( category = c("middle", "right", "left"),
  count = c(150, 307, 205))

rows <- 20

waffle_bears <- bearswaf |>
  uncount(count) |>
  mutate(
    x = (row_number() - 1) %/% rows + 1,
    y = (row_number() - 1) %% rows + 1
  )

ggplot() +
  geom_tile(
    data=waffle_bears, 
    aes(
      x = x, y = y, fill = category
      ), 
    color = "white", 
    linewidth = .5) +
  coord_equal() + 
  theme_void()









The Bears have favored to throw the ball to the right side of the field.


Code
ggplot(bearspass, aes(x = week)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = pass_attempt), fill = "navy") +
  geom_col(aes(y = complete_pass), fill = "orange") +
  labs(
    title = "Attempts vs Completions Each Week",
    x = "Week",
    y = "Completed vs Attempts"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









The Chicago Bears passing game never showed consitency throughout the season. Throught the weeks completions make up a steady perportion of attempts, but the gap is still promanate. Later in the season we see a surge in attempts but we dont see the same surge in completed passes. Therefore, as time goes on the Bears performance seems to reset every week. This indicates the Bears relied on quantity and chance rather than acuuracy and execution. Overall, this chart shows a passing game that struggled to find a consistant identity.
